data.title: Insteel signals robust 2026 demand outlook with $20M capex plan and data center growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insteel Industries Inc. (IIIN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • H.O. Waltz, President, CEO & Chairman, stated that "the upturn in business activity we reported previously continued during our first quarter, and our fiscal 2025 acquisitions continue to perform well." He expressed optimism for 2026, saying, "we are encouraged by the level of optimism in our markets as well as brisk order entry up to this point in January that causes us to believe that 2026 will be a strong year for the company."
  • CEO Waltz highlighted uncertainty in the market environment, noting, "we are aware of uncertainties created by the administration's trade policies the nation's fiscal conditions and by the economic cycle."
  • Scot Jafroodi, VP, CFO & Treasurer, reported, "First quarter results benefited from improved demand for our concrete enforcing products which support a wider spreads between selling prices and raw material costs. Net earnings for the quarter rose to $7.6 million or $0.39 per share compared with $1.1 million or $0.06 per share in the same period last year."
  • CFO Jafroodi also commented, "Average selling prices increased 18.8% year-over-year, this reflects the pricing actions we took throughout fiscal 2025 to offset higher steel wire rod costs... Sequentially, average selling prices were essentially unchanged from the fourth quarter as we did not take additional pricing actions during the current period. However, with scrap and wire rod prices now moving higher again, we implemented our oil price increases across most product lines, which took effect earlier this month."
  • CEO Waltz detailed raw material strategy, stating, "Our inventories have grown because we are unable to acquire sufficient quantities of wire rod domestically, and we are forced to go offshore... we'll continue doing so until such time as we see that availability improves in the U.S. and suppliers, again, are willing to work for an order."
  • CEO Waltz outlined capital deployment, saying, "we expect to invest approximately $20 million in our plants and information systems infrastructure during 2026. You can expect our investments to support the growth of our engineered structural mesh business to reduce our cash production costs and to enhance the robust nature of our information systems."

Outlook

  • CEO Waltz signaled confidence for 2026, stating, "we're pleased with the acceleration of business activity that continued through our first quarter... see no indication that the level of activity in our markets is poised to subside."
  • Management expects the demand uptick, partly attributed to Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funding and data center construction, to "remain robust into 2027."
  • Jafroodi projected, "we expect our effective tax rate for the remainder of the year to be approximately 23%."
  • Inventory levels are expected to moderate over the course of the second quarter as purchasing activity normalizes and shipment volumes increase.

Financial Results

  • CFO Jafroodi reported, "First quarter shipments... increased 3.8% year-over-year. On a sequential basis, shipments declined 9.7% from the fourth quarter which is consistent with normal seasonal patterns."
  • Gross profit for the quarter improved to $18.1 million from $9.5 million a year ago, with gross margin expanding 400 basis points to 11.3% from 7.3%. Sequentially, gross profit declined by $10.5 million from the fourth quarter and gross margin narrowed by 480 basis points.
  • SG&A expenses for the quarter rose to $8.8 million or 5.5% of net sales, compared with $7.9 million or 6.1% of net sales in the prior year, primarily due to a compensation expense increase.
  • Cash flow from operations used $700,000 in the quarter, compared to providing $19 million last year, with net working capital using $16.6 million in cash.
  • The company returned $19.4 million to shareholders through a special cash dividend and continued share buybacks of $745,000.
  • Insteel ended the quarter with $15.6 million in cash on hand and no borrowings on its $100 million revolving credit facility.

Q&A

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked about data center demand and pipeline commitments. H.O. Waltz responded, "the data center business is new in Insteel... now that we're in that market and connected with some of the customers that regularly do that business, we're seeing repeat opportunities and robust demand."
  • Romero questioned the impact of wire rod constraints on volumes. Waltz explained, "our inventories have grown because we are unable to acquire sufficient quantities of wire rod domestically, and we are forced to go offshore... and we'll continue doing so until such time as we see that availability improves in the U.S."
  • Romero asked about SG&A leverage from acquisitions. Waltz replied, "we've certainly realized the synergies we expected to come from the acquisition."
  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked about the company outperforming industry stats and the role of ESM (engineered structural mesh) products. Waltz replied, "our work in the cast-in-place market has helped. Our acquisitions have helped."
  • Bauer inquired about inventory levels and potential downtick. Waltz said, "if the scenario that we believe will unfold... unfolds... then I think that's correct. But... we will go back to the offshore market for Q3 and 4 if we don't see significant improvements in the balance of supply and demand domestically."
  • Bauer also asked about CapEx allocation. Waltz replied, "I'd say that's close to correct. We're still identifying some of the capacity expansion opportunities... we're always interested in incorporating new technology into our manufacturing operations that will help us reduce the cash cost of operation."
  • Bauer asked about residential market potential. Waltz responded, "probably not fast enough to have any meaningful impact on 2026 for Insteel."
  • Bauer questioned labor cost outlook. Waltz stated, "the upward pressure on labor cost, still exists... The inflationary environment is alive and well within our operations."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on demand visibility, wire rod supply constraints, and leverage from acquisitions, while probing for clarity on costs and capital allocation.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and constructive during prepared remarks, but became more measured in the Q&A, especially when addressing supply chain, inventory, and labor cost pressures.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone remained steady, while management’s confidence was similarly high, though with greater emphasis on supply chain complexities and inflation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company continued to emphasize strong demand and successful integration of 2025 acquisitions, similar to Q4 2025.
  • Guidance language remained optimistic, with a stronger focus on data center demand and Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funding as drivers for 2026 and beyond.
  • Management highlighted increased raw material costs and ongoing need to source from offshore suppliers, an escalation from last quarter’s discussion of domestic supply constraints.
  • Inventory levels rose further compared to Q4, reflecting continued offshore purchases; SG&A and compensation expenses also increased.
  • The tone on inflation and labor costs became more prominent, while capital allocation priorities were consistent with the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Waltz and CFO Jafroodi cited uncertainties stemming from U.S. trade policies, specifically Section 232 tariffs and their impact on steel wire rod prices and availability.
  • Management discussed ongoing inflationary pressures, including labor, energy, and spare parts costs.
  • Analysts raised concerns about inventory build, supply chain reliability, and the speed of any potential residential market recovery, with management responding that residential is unlikely to materially impact fiscal 2026 results.

Final Takeaway

Insteel Industries delivered a strong start to fiscal 2026, underpinned by robust demand in commercial and infrastructure segments and supported by continued integration of recent acquisitions. Management anticipates that demand linked to infrastructure funding and data center construction will remain strong into 2027, while also acknowledging ongoing risks from supply chain constraints, inflation, and trade policy volatility. The company remains focused on driving operational efficiencies, pursuing growth investments—including a $20 million capex plan—and maintaining capital returns to shareholders, positioning itself to navigate market uncertainties and capitalize on emerging opportunities in its end markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Coterra Energy exploring potential merger deal with Devon Energy - Bloomberg
data.description:
Coterra Energy (CTRA) +3.2% in Thursday's trading following a Bloomberg report that it is holding talks with Devon Energy (DVN) about a potential merger, a deal that would rank among the biggest oil and gas deals in years.

The companies - both with large positions in the Permian Basin - are still negotiating the terms and structure of a potential deal, according to the report, which also said Coterra (CTRA) has held merger talks with at least one other company in the recent past.

Devon (DVN) owns ~400K net acres in the Permian's Delaware Basin, where Coterra (CTRA) also has a 346K-acre position, and a deal would enable the combined company to better compete with Permian giants such as Exxon Mobil and Diamondback Energy.

Devon (DVN) also has drilling operations in the Rocky Mountain region, southeast Texas and Oklahoma, while Coterra’s (CTRA) other operations cover western Oklahoma as well as Pennsylvania's Marcellus Shale.

data.title: Aurora Innovation points to real-world success with its self-driving technology
data.description:
Aurora Innovation (AUR) took a step forward in its commercialization efforts with the completed integration with McLeod Software's transportation management system.McLeod Software finished the API integration ahead of schedule, and eligible McLeod TMS customers can now tender, dispatch, and track Aurora-powered autonomous loads directly inside the software they already use every day. McLeod is positioning itself as the first major TMS to put autonomous trucking “directly into the hands” of its customers, which effectively turns Aurora’s Driver into a native option inside a widely adopted freight operating system. 

The strong inbound interest from McLeod's customer base could signal real demand for autonomous capacity and supports the bullish outlook on Aurora Innovation that it can generate revenue from existing freight workflows.​ By embedding Aurora Driver as a turnkey option in McLeod’s ecosystem, Aurora appears to be moving from "pilot tech projects" toward a repeatable service product that carriers can adopt at scale. For instance, Russell Transport, a premier carrier and longtime McLeod customer, is utilizing the feature to tender autonomous loads powered by the Aurora Driver.

Shares of Aurora Innovation (AUR) are up more than 20% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Ford exploring hybrid battery partnership with China's BYD -- report
data.description:
• To supply its hybrid vehicle production and replace terminated agreements with LG Energy Solutions and SK On, Ford (F) is turning to China’s number one automaker for batteries, according to sources cited by The Wall Street Journal.
  • The two companies are reportedly in discussions in which Ford (F) would buy batteries manufactured by BYD (BYDDY) (BYDDF) for its hybrid vehicles. Under one discussed scenario, Ford (F) would import BYD (BYDDY) (BYDDF) batteries to Ford’s (F) overseas factories.
  • Prior to making cars, BYD (BYDDY) (BYDDF) manufactured batteries for electric vehicles, producing most at facilities within China but recently expanding overseas.
  • As the company pivots from fully electric vehicles to hybrids and gas-powered cars, the need for hybrid-specific batteries has grown, leading the company to build its own battery-producing plant in Michigan using technology developed by China’s CATL.

data.title: Critical Metals launches 50-50 Saudi JV to refine rare earths from Greenland
data.description:
Critical Metals (CRML) said Thursday it signed a non-binding term sheet to form a 50-50 joint venture with Saudi Arabia's Tariq Abdel Hadi Abdullah Al-Qahtani & Brothers Company that would refine 25% of capacity from the planned Tanbreez rare earth project in Greenland in the kingdom.

Critical Metals (CRML) said the term sheet outlines the framework for the development, financing, construction, and operation of a state-of-the-art rare earth processing facility in Saudi Arabia to create a fully integrated mine-to-processing supply chain, including long-term offtake rights for 25% of the Tanbreez rare earth concentrate production to Saudi Arabia.

The agreement, if finalized, would give Critical Metals (CRML) guaranteed customers and likely ease its path to full financing for the project, which is estimated to cost $290M to start production by next year, Reuters reported; the company already has pre-sold 75% of planned output, meaning production in Greenland is spoken for.

"This agreement represents a transformational milestone for Critical Metals Corp," Chairman Tony Sage said. "By partnering with a leading Saudi Arabian industrial group and securing long-term offtake that brings Tanbreez to 100% committed production, we have effectively de-risked the project's commercial pathway from mine to market."

Critical Metals (CRML) shares -4.4% in Thursday's trading, pulling back after surging by nearly a third in the previous session after reporting strong assay results in its 2025 drilling program that confirmed the presence of strategic metals across its Tanbreez project.

data.title: Aerospace earnings season approaches with strong backdrop, Susquehanna says
data.description:
U.S. aerospace and defense companies are entering the fourth quarter earnings season with favorable fundamentals across commercial aviation, defense and aftermarket services, according to a research note on Thursday from Susquehanna Financial Group.

The firm said it has raised price targets across much of its coverage universe, citing improving production stability at aircraft manufacturers, resilient aftermarket demand and sustained government defense spending. The outlook applies to a broad group of companies including Boeing (BA), GE Aerospace (GE) and Raytheon Technologies (RTX).

In commercial aerospace, the analyst said Boeing (BA) has made meaningful progress in stabilizing production on its 737 and 787 programs. With the Spirit AeroSystems acquisition completed and production recovering, investor focus is expected to shift toward 777X certification and the pace of free cash flow improvement over the next several years.

Susquehanna added that stronger conditions at original equipment manufacturers are benefiting the broader supply chain. The firm expects improving revenue visibility and margin expansion to support earnings and free cash flow growth in 2026 and 2027, particularly for suppliers and engine makers.

On the defense side, the research note points to multiple tailwinds. These include several years of book-to-bill ratios above one, a U.S. administration supportive of higher defense budgets and commitments by NATO allies to increase military spending. Recent legislation also reinforces the outlook for sustained growth, the analyst said.

“We believe we are in the midst of a highly favorable aerospace and defense spending upcycle,” wrote analyst Charles P. Minervino, citing improving visibility and rising free cash flow across much of the sector.

While valuations across the sector have expanded, Susquehanna cautioned that near-term earnings results may face heightened scrutiny. Still, the firm said the medium-term setup remains attractive, supported by strong demand trends and improving operational execution.

Positive-rated on (ATI), (BA), (GD), (GE), (HWM), (LHX), (LMT), (NOC), (RTX), (SARO) and (TXT).

Neutral-rated on (SPCE) and (TDG).

data.title: Market Voices: Goolsbee on Trump vs. Fed, Ford on trade, Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said that the Trump administration's efforts to undermine the Fed's independence could end up stoking inflation. 

“Anything that’s infringing or attacking the independence of the central bank is a mess,” Goolsbee said during an interview with CNBC. “You’re going to get inflation come roaring back if you try to take away the independence of the central bank.”

  • Ford CEO Jim Farley said that the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement urgently needs to be updated.

“We built our entire vehicle business as an industry between Canada, Mexico, and the U.S.,” Farley said Thursday during a Bloomberg TV interview. “We have to get this revised.”

President Trump said earlier this week that he had little interest in renegotiating the agreement, calling it "irrelevant," according to Bloomberg.  

  • Goldman Sachs (GS) CEO David Solomon said his bank is exploring opportunities in the prediction markets, which he called "super interesting."

“We have a team of people here that are spending time with them and are looking at it,” Solomon said on a call with analysts Thursday, according to Bloomberg.

"I can certainly see opportunities where these cross into our business,” Solomon added, referring to prediction markets contracts.

data.title: Samsara gets rating upgrade at BNP Paribas
data.description:
BNP Paribas upgraded Samsara's (IOT) rating to Outperform from Neutral with a $40 price target, citing improved risk/reward.

Shares of Samsara — which provides a Connected Operations Platform that aggregates data from its Internet of Things, or IoT, devices — rose about 1% on Thursday.

"After a tough year, we are becoming more constructive on IOT shares, as we think the demand for Samsara's technology will be robust this year and are not concerned about rising competition upmarket. Meanwhile, we think its base of installed hardware will likely limit its replaceability by AI," said analyst Andrew DeGasperi.

The analysts noted that demand appears to have stabilized after a tough first half of 2025 marked by the disruption brought on by tariffs that led to extended sales cycles at some of their customers.

DeGasperi added that while they had flagged earlier last year, post-election of the risks to the company's business, the fiscal second quarter, and the fiscal third quarter net new annual recurring revenue, or NNARR, growth reaccelerated, and they see long-term tailwinds from customers adjusting their supply chains.

"While we see rising competition upmarket from companies like Motive, we believe that in an underpenetrated market, Samsara's early mover advantage with large fleets and scale in go-to market may limit share loss. Further, after reviewing Motive's S-1 filed last month, we see little room for proceeds beyond paying off debt. Lastly, our checks found that Samsara's hardware may not be as commoditized as is perceived in the market, particularly outside of telematics and being embedded in the asset limits displacement risks by AI," said DeGasperi.

The analyst noted that with top-line growth now stabilizing in the mid-high 20% range, they believe the risk/reward is attractive.

OneStream (OS) 

BNP Paribas downgraded OneStream (OS) to Neutral and lowered the price target to $24, following an announcement last week that Hg Capital is acquiring the company for $6.4B.

BNP Paribas' top ideas for 2026

"After a tough 2025 for software, we believe investors need to remain selective. For 2026, we recommend investors remain tactical and pick stocks with strong fundamentals while avoiding names that may be highly correlated to AI. In this report, we also provide a more granular analysis of hiring trends across our coverage and what it means for top-line acceleration/margin expansion," said DeGasperi.

The firm's top pick is Veeva Systems (VEEV).

DeGasperi said that despite strong execution through 2025, Veeva's shares underperformed in the last few weeks on risks to its CRM business. However, he added that valuation is attractive, demand trends for clinical and marketing products are strong, and risks of disruption from AI are minimal.

BNP Paribas also likes HubSpot (HUBS) and Zscaler (ZS), citing potential growth acceleration in fiscal 2026.

Vertex (VERX): The analyst noted that Vertex is likely to see an uptick in growth from easier comps and the adoption of e-invoicing in Europe.

data.title: Snap set to continue losses for seven straight sessions
data.description:
Snap (SNAP) shares are on track to continue losses for seven straight sessions, as the stock fell 1.6% at $7.78 in afternoon trading on Thursday.

The Snapchat-owner lost over 8% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock lost over 28% last year, compared to the 15% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

Santa Monica, California-based Snap is up over 5% over the past one month. The stock closed 1% lower on Thursday at $7.78.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, SNAP has a Buy rating with a score of 4.11 out of 5. The company received an A in the prospect of growth, while it got a D in the valuation factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, seven analysts gave SNAP a Buy and above, 33 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and four recommended Sell or lower.

Seeking Alpha analysts also see the stock as a Hold.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis on SNAP said valuation and margins are in line with industry peers, limiting upside unless SNAP unlocks a new revenue growth cycle.

Earlier in November, the company exceeded expectations for top-and-bottom line numbers and most key metrics in the third quarter. However, its new $400M partnership with Perplexity to integrate an AI search chatbot into Snapchat impressed investors.

“SNAP's partnership with Perplexity, set to deliver $400 million in 2026, could catalyze margin expansion and accelerate top-line growth,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Julian Lin.

data.title: Eli Lilly drops as FDA decision on weight loss pill is now expected in April
data.description:
Eli Lilly (LLY) shares slipped ~5% on Thursday, marking what could be its biggest intraday loss in five months after Reuters reported that a potential FDA nod for the company’s weight loss pill, orforglipron, is now due April 10, a delayed timeline compared to LLY’s initial expectations.

The report highlighted review delays faced by two recipients of FDA Commissioner’s National Priority Review vouchers. Unveiled in June, the pilot program aims to serve companies aligning with U.S. national priorities to expedite the review of their experimental therapies from 10–12 months to just 1–2 months.

When the FDA issued a National Priority Review voucher for Lilly’s (LLY) oral GLP-1 agonist in November, the company’s CEO, David Ricks, said that the once-daily pill could be approved as early as Q1 2026.

However, CFO Lucas Montarce tempered expectations this week, noting that the company is on track for orforglipron’s approval in Q2 2026, a decision that could pave the way for the latest entrant in the promising market of oral obesity drugs.

Last week, rival Novo Nordisk (NVO) launched its Wegovy pill for cash-paying customers in the U.S. at $149 per month after the oral version of its popular injectable semaglutide was approved by the FDA last month.
